title: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

reviews.summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.
reviews.questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary:

The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient).

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.

Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.

Questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?

The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: PAC Prediction Sets for Few-Shot Meta-Learning

## Summary

This paper presents an extension of PAC prediction sets to the few-shot meta-learning setting, introducing Meta-PAC sets that provide task-conditional coverage guarantees across multiple future tasks. The work builds upon Meta-CP by offering high-probability guarantees that hold on average over new tasks rather than just marginalizing over task distributions. The paper is generally well-written with solid theoretical foundations and demonstrates strong empirical performance across diverse domains. However, there are notable limitations regarding presentation clarity, practical applicability, and the degree of novelty in the theoretical contribution.

## Strengths

The paper makes a meaningful contribution to an important problem. The theoretical setting is appealing and well-motivated—providing guarantees that hold across multiple future tasks conditional on observed calibration tasks represents a useful extension over prior Meta-CP work. The authors clearly articulate how Meta-PAC sets differ from existing approaches, particularly highlighting that the guarantees apply on average across all new tasks rather than just marginalizing over individual task draws.

The calibration strategy is elegant and theoretically sound. By recognizing that only the i.i.d. property of PAC outputs is required (rather than simultaneous validity across all tasks), the authors avoid an inefficient 1/N union bound factor. Instead, they employ a two-stage approach using two 1/2 factors, which is more efficient and avoids unnecessary looseness in the bounds.

From a practical standpoint, the ability to reuse a single fixed threshold across multiple future tasks without requiring additional calibration data is genuinely useful. This addresses a real limitation of task-specific approaches and simplifies deployment.

The empirical validation is comprehensive and convincing. Results across four datasets spanning three distinct domains (vision with mini-ImageNet and CIFAR10-C, language with FewRel, and medical imaging with CDC Heart Dataset) demonstrate the method's broad applicability. The experiments are well-designed with appropriate numbers of random trials (100 for calibration, 50 for test datasets).

## Weaknesses

The presentation could be substantially improved. Section 4 launches directly into heavy notation without sufficient intuitive motivation. Readers would benefit from a high-level, step-by-step explanation of what the authors aim to accomplish before diving into formal definitions. The paper would also gain clarity by explicitly articulating the four sources of averaging/high probability in Meta-PAC sets: (1) the random draw of calibration tasks, (2) the random choice of test task, (3) predictions on new tasks, and (4) the prediction set containing the correct label. Lines 171-174 regarding the score function g and the dummy label convention particularly need clarification.

The theoretical contribution, while correct, is relatively incremental. The approach essentially combines existing techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration) in a straightforward manner. While the combination is non-trivial, the conceptual novelty is moderate, which may limit the paper's broader impact.

A significant practical limitation is the requirement for several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks. This assumption is quite restrictive and unrealistic for most real-world meta-learning scenarios. The paper would benefit from discussing when this assumption is reasonable and when it becomes prohibitive.

The use of a fixed, non-adaptive threshold across all downstream tasks represents a departure from Meta-CP's data-dependent approach. While this simplifies deployment, it may be suboptimal when there is substantial variability across tasks. Meta-CP's ability to estimate task-specific thresholds could provide better performance in such settings. The authors do not adequately discuss whether the threshold could be adaptively updated given sufficient adaptation data from new tasks.

The tightness of the Clopper-Pearson bounds with small calibration task sizes remains unclear. Given that small calibration sets are common in practice, empirical investigation or theoretical analysis of bound tightness would strengthen the work.

Finally, the paper could expand its discussion of limitations. The guarantee that Meta-PAC sets are valid "on average across new tasks" rather than for individual tasks is a fundamental restriction that should be more prominently acknowledged. This is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation and deserves clearer positioning.

## Minor Suggestions

1. Add a high-level algorithmic description at the beginning of Section 4, explaining the overall strategy before introducing formal notation.

2. Explicitly enumerate and explain the four sources of averaging/high probability in the Meta-PAC framework.

3. Provide clearer exposition of the score function definition (lines 171-174) and the dummy label convention used in the second-stage calibration.

4. Investigate or discuss the empirical tightness of Clopper-Pearson bounds when calibration task sizes are small (e.g., 10-50 tasks instead of several hundred).

5. Expand the conclusion to more explicitly discuss the strong assumptions required by the method and its limitations as a general solution for few-shot confidence estimation.

6. Consider whether adaptive threshold updates might be feasible given sufficient adaptation data, and discuss the trade-offs involved.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents a technically sound extension of PAC prediction sets to meta-learning with practical utility. The theoretical contribution is solid though somewhat incremental, and the empirical results are convincing across multiple domains. The main limitations stem from presentation clarity, restrictive assumptions about calibration data availability, and the non-adaptive nature of the fixed threshold approach. Despite these concerns, the work makes a meaningful contribution to an important problem and demonstrates practical effectiveness.

The paper merits acceptance as a solid contribution that advances the field, though it falls short of being a strong accept due to moderate novelty and practical limitations that should be more thoroughly discussed.

extracted_ideas.summary: This paper extends PAC prediction sets to few-shot meta-learning settings, offering a meaningful contribution by providing task-conditional guarantees that differ from prior Meta-CP work. The paper is generally well-written with solid theory and strong empirical results, though presentation could be improved and some practical limitations exist.
strengths: Well-written paper that clearly explains the relationship between PAC sets and Meta-PAC sets as an extension of Meta-CP sets

Theoretical setting is appealing and represents a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP by providing high probability guarantees that hold across multiple future tasks rather than just marginalized over task draws
The calibration strategy is correct and elegant, avoiding the need for a 1/N union bound factor by leveraging the i.i.d. property of PAC method outputs rather than requiring simultaneous validity
Empirical results are strong across four datasets spanning three application domains (vision, language, and medical)
Practical advantage that a single threshold can be reused across future tasks without requiring additional calibration data
The paper does a good job pointing out desirable guarantees not covered by Meta-CP, particularly that Meta-PAC sets are valid on average across new tasks
weaknesses: Presentation is burdensome with heavy notation that could be simplified; jumps directly into notation in Section 4 without high-level intuitive explanation

Single threshold is used for all downstream tasks and never gets updated regardless of adaptation set size, unlike Meta-CP which estimates data-dependent thresholds that could be better when there is significant variability across tasks
Tightness of Clopper-Pearson based bounds with small calibration task sizes is unclear
Requiring access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems unrealistic apart from very specific scenarios
Theory is a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration), limiting novelty and anticipated impact
Limitations could be expanded regarding the assumption that guarantees only hold on average across new tasks, not for specific individual tasks
suggestions: Provide a high-level, step-by-step description at the start of Section 4 explaining what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, before diving into notation

Use intuitive terms to explain the four sources of averaging/high probability in Meta-PAC: (1) over random draw of calibration tasks, (2) over random choice of test task, (3) over predictions on new task, and (4) the prediction set contains correct label

Expand discussion in conclusion about the limitations that the algorithm relies on several strong assumptions and only holds on average across new tasks, not as a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation
Clarify the explanation of lines 171-174 regarding the score function g and dummy label convention
Investigate or discuss the tightness of Clopper-Pearson bounds in practical scenarios with smaller calibration task sizes
Consider addressing whether the single fixed threshold could be adaptively updated given sufficient adaptation data from new tasks
main_points: The paper extends PAC prediction sets to meta-learning by providing high probability guarantees over future tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks, which differs from Meta-CP's marginal coverage guarantees

Meta-PAC sets are valid on average across all new tasks rather than just marginalized over the (n+1)th task draw, making them more practical for reusing fixed calibration sets across multiple future tasks
The two-stage calibration approach (computing PAC thresholds for each calibration task, then computing a PAC interval over these thresholds) is theoretically sound and avoids inefficient union bounds
The approach uses a fixed, non-adaptive threshold across all downstream tasks, which differs from Meta-CP's data-dependent threshold approach
Strong empirical validation across diverse domains (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel, CDC Heart Dataset) demonstrates practical effectiveness
The theoretical contribution, while solid, represents a relatively straightforward combination of existing techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration)
technical_details: The algorithm avoids needing a 1/N union bound factor by recognizing that only the i.i.d. property of PAC outputs is needed, not simultaneous validity of all individual task thresholds
The calibration strategy uses two 1/2 factors (α/2 for each stage) instead of a single 1/N factor, making it more efficient
Clopper-Pearson bounds are used for the PAC prediction set construction (Algorithm 2 in Appendix B)
The score function g is defined as g(τ, y) := 1(y = 1) to convert threshold values into a binary classification problem for the second-stage PAC calibration
A dummy label of 1 is used to fit the convention of the PAC prediction set algorithm when creating the sample S' of estimated thresholds
Experiments use 100 random trials for calibration datasets and 50 random trials for test datasets per calibration set to evaluate the three-parameter guarantee
The practical metric evaluates empirical prediction set error as 1/|E| Σ(y ∉ F^A_τ(x)) for fixed calibration and test datasets

Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Summary:
This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

## Summary

This paper presents a PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) prediction set algorithm designed to enable predictors to adapt to new tasks with limited labeled examples in meta-learning settings. The work combines theoretical analysis with empirical validation across multiple domains. While the paper demonstrates technical soundness and reasonable experimental breadth, there are notable limitations regarding the scope of applicability and the depth of experimental evaluation.

## Strengths

The paper is well-written and clearly presented, making it accessible to readers. The proposed method is reasonable in its formulation, and the authors provide theoretical analysis that grounds their approach. Additionally, the experimental validation spans diverse domains and datasets, which demonstrates an effort to evaluate the method broadly across different problem settings.

## Major Weaknesses

**1. Parameter Adaptation Strategy**

A significant design choice in the paper is the reuse of meta-learned prediction set parameters without adaptation during the task-specific adaptation phase. While this simplifies implementation, it raises questions about optimality. The authors do not adequately justify why fixed parameters should remain optimal after adapting the base predictor to a new task. This design choice warrants deeper investigation or reconsideration.

**2. Limited Scope to Metric-Based Methods**

The current approach is restricted to metric-based meta-learning methods, with prototypical networks serving as the primary demonstration. Notably, the method does not extend to optimization-based approaches such as MAML, which represent an important class of meta-learning algorithms. This limitation significantly constrains the practical applicability and generalizability of the work. The inability to handle gradient-based meta-learning approaches is a considerable gap.

**3. Experimental Evaluation Scope**

While experiments span multiple domains, the complexity of the classification tasks evaluated is relatively limited. Specifically, the mini-ImageNet experiments focus exclusively on 5-way classification, which is a standard but not particularly challenging setting. The evaluation would be substantially strengthened by including more demanding scenarios such as 10-way or 20-way classification, which would better demonstrate the method's capabilities and robustness.

## Minor Concerns

The paper's theoretical contribution is sound, but given the restriction to metric-based methods, the practical impact may be more limited than initially suggested. The experimental results, while positive, do not fully explore the boundaries of where the method performs well or poorly.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Parameter Adaptation**: Investigate whether adapting the meta prediction set parameters during the task-specific adaptation phase would improve performance. Provide theoretical or empirical justification for the current choice to reuse fixed parameters.

2. **Extension to Optimization-Based Methods**: Develop extensions that enable the method to work with optimization-based meta-learning algorithms like MAML. This would substantially broaden the applicability of the work.

3. **Challenging Classification Settings**: Include experiments on more difficult classification tasks beyond 5-way, such as 10-way or 20-way classification on mini-ImageNet, to better demonstrate the method's effectiveness under more demanding conditions.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a technically solid contribution to meta-learning with PAC prediction sets. The theoretical analysis is sound, and the experimental validation demonstrates competence. However, the restriction to metric-based methods and the relatively straightforward experimental settings limit the scope and demonstrated impact. The design choice regarding parameter adaptation also requires clarification. Despite these limitations, the work is sufficiently developed and the ideas are reasonable enough to warrant publication. The paper would benefit from addressing the suggested improvements in future work.

**Recommendation: Weak Accept**

The paper is technically competent with no major flaws in evaluation, reproducibility, or ethical considerations. While it has notable limitations in scope and generalizability, it presents a reasonable contribution that would be of interest to the meta-learning community.

reviews.summary: This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Meta-PAC Framework for Prediction Sets in Meta-Learning

## Summary

This paper introduces a meta-PAC (mPAC) framework that extends classical PAC guarantees for prediction sets to the meta-learning setting, where predictions must be made across multiple distributions with access to adaptation data. The authors formalize the mPAC notion, present theoretical guarantees, and propose an algorithmic solution that leverages existing PAC prediction set methods.

## Strengths

The paper makes a solid contribution by extending PAC prediction set theory to an underexplored but practically relevant setting. The generalization of conformal prediction principles from single-distribution to multi-distribution meta-learning scenarios is non-trivial and well-motivated. The theoretical framework is technically sound, with clear analysis and rigorous proofs establishing the mPAC guarantees in Theorem 1.

The presentation is commendable—the paper is well-written and easy to follow, with good intuition provided for the main concepts. The mPAC notion (Definition 3) is clearly defined and appropriately accounts for the randomness sources inherent in meta-learning: augmented calibration sets across N distributions and test adaptation sets.

The algorithmic approach is practical, building sensibly upon existing PAC prediction set algorithms rather than attempting unnecessary novelty. The two-step strategy elegantly decomposes the problem: first constructing (ε, α/2)-PAC estimators on individual distributions, then combining them into a meta-level prediction set. Additionally, the authors demonstrate appropriate care in addressing limitations and potential societal impacts of their work.

## Weaknesses

The primary limitation concerns the computational efficiency of the proposed solution. Algorithm 2 employs a grid search procedure to determine the threshold parameter τ, but the paper provides insufficient analysis of the associated computational costs. Specifically:

1. **Lack of complexity analysis**: No formal computational complexity analysis is provided for the grid search procedure, making it difficult to assess scalability properties.

2. **Grid granularity not addressed**: The paper does not discuss how the granularity of the grid search should be chosen, nor does it provide guidance on selecting an appropriate grid resolution that balances accuracy and computational cost.

3. **Limited experimental validation**: While the theoretical framework is sound, the paper would benefit from empirical evaluation demonstrating the practical efficiency of the grid search approach, particularly as the number of distributions N or the dimensionality increases.

## Minor Suggestions for Improvement

To strengthen the paper, I recommend:

1. Provide explicit computational complexity analysis for Algorithm 2, characterizing the cost as a function of problem parameters (N, sample sizes, grid resolution).

2. Discuss principled approaches for selecting grid granularity, potentially with theoretical or empirical justification.

3. Explore potential acceleration strategies for the grid search, such as binary search variants or adaptive sampling methods that could reduce computational overhead.

4. Include experiments that measure wall-clock time and computational requirements, particularly comparing performance across different problem scales.

## Technical Comments

The core technical contribution is sound. The framework appropriately differs from prior work (e.g., meta conformal prediction [14]) by conditioning on adaptation sets, thereby providing guarantees specifically tailored to adapted prediction sets rather than pre-adaptation scenarios. The parameter splitting strategy (dividing δ into δ/2 for each step) is well-justified. The assumption that adaptation datasets are available without requiring importance weight estimation is practical and distinguishes this work from covariate shift settings.

## Overall Assessment

This is a technically solid paper that addresses a relevant problem at the intersection of meta-learning and conformal prediction. The theoretical contributions are meaningful, the presentation is clear, and the work demonstrates appropriate scholarly care. The main limitation—insufficient treatment of computational efficiency—is not fundamental to the contribution but would strengthen the paper's practical applicability. The work makes a moderate-to-high impact contribution to an important area with no major concerns regarding evaluation rigor, reproducibility, or ethical considerations.

**Recommendation: Weak Accept**

The paper merits publication as a solid theoretical contribution with practical algorithmic solutions. Addressing the computational efficiency concerns in a revision would further enhance its value to the community.

Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.
reviews.questions: What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?
reviews.limitations: I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that. reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Training-Conditional Guarantees for Meta-Learning

## Summary

This paper extends training-conditional prediction sets to the meta-learning setting, proposing a two-stage algorithm that provides formal guarantees on the validity of predicted sets. While the technical contribution is sound and the experimental evaluation is comprehensive, the paper suffers from significant presentation challenges and attribution issues that diminish its accessibility and impact.

## Strengths

1. **Technical Soundness**: The proofs are correct and the theoretical results are rigorously established. The paper makes a genuine contribution to the theory of meta-learning by providing the first two-stage training-conditional guarantee in this setting.

2. **Algorithmic Innovation**: The approach of applying binomial concentration twice sequentially is clever and well-motivated, allowing the authors to condition on additional variables beyond what previous methods achieve.

3. **Comprehensive Experimental Validation**: The experiments span multiple domains (vision, language, medicine) and evaluate performance across several meta-learning tasks, demonstrating the breadth of the approach.

4. **Accurate Related Work**: The related work section properly positions the contribution within the existing literature on conformal prediction and meta-learning.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Presentation Complexity**: The paper is dense and difficult to follow, employing heavy mathematical notation that will limit its accessibility to practitioners outside the specialized PAC guarantees community. The core guarantee involves three nested parameters (ε, α, δ) that are complex to interpret and set in practice.

2. **Unclear Practical Applicability**: The cognitive burden of selecting three parameters may prove prohibitive for real-world adoption. The paper does not provide sufficient guidance on how practitioners should set these parameters in concrete applications, nor does it discuss realistic scenarios where the i.i.d. task assumption holds.

3. **Attribution and Terminology Issues**: The paper contains significant attribution problems. The concept of training-conditional validity originates from Vovk [21], not from "PAC prediction sets [3]" as suggested in several places. Additionally, the PS-BINOM algorithm should be credited to Vovk [21] Proposition 2b rather than presented as a novel contribution. The rebranding of established terminology obscures the true origins of the work.

4. **Presentation of Technical Content**: Section 4.1 attempts to convey too much information simultaneously, making it difficult to extract key insights. Figure 2 lacks essential clarity—color distinctions are missing, stripe orientations are unexplained, and notation (such as the meaning of the 'N' box) is unclear.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Incremental Technical Contribution**: While correct, applying existing binomial concentration techniques twice rather than once represents a relatively straightforward extension of prior work. The novelty is moderate.

2. **Limited Advantage Over Baselines**: The meta-conformal prediction baseline [14] already provides working guarantees. While the proposed approach conditions on adaptation sets rather than entire test tasks, the practical advantage appears marginal, particularly given the added complexity.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Correct Attribution**: Remove all references to "PAC prediction sets [3]" as the origin of training-conditional validity. Credit Vovk [21] as the original source throughout the paper. Clarify that the PS-BINOM algorithm corresponds to Vovk [21] Proposition 2b.

2. **Improve Presentation of Technical Content**: Reorganize Section 4.1 to present information more gradually and digestibly. Enhance Figure 2 by adding color coding, explaining stripe orientations, and clarifying all notation.

3. **Enhance Practical Guidance**: Provide concrete examples and intuitive explanations for how practitioners should select the three parameters. Include a discussion of realistic meta-learning scenarios where the i.i.d. task assumption is reasonable.

4. **Broaden Accessibility**: Simplify the exposition where possible, and consider adding intuitive explanations alongside formal results to make the work accessible to a broader audience.

## Technical Assessment

The guarantee structure—triply-nested with innermost expectation over 0-1 loss, nested within probabilities conditioned on calibration set and meta-learned parameters—is technically correct. The use of sequential binomial concentration is sound, though straightforward. Experiments using prototypical networks as score functions are appropriate for the few-shot learning setting.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a legitimate theoretical contribution to meta-learning by extending training-conditional guarantees to this setting. The technical results are correct, the experiments are thorough, and the work advances our understanding of formal guarantees in meta-learning. However, the presentation is unnecessarily complex, attribution issues must be resolved, and practical guidance for parameter selection is insufficient. These issues prevent the work from achieving its full potential impact.

The paper is technically solid and addresses a relevant problem, but presentation and accessibility concerns limit its scope. With revisions addressing the attribution issues and improving clarity, this work would be stronger.

**Recommendation: Weak Accept**

The paper merits publication due to its technical correctness and comprehensive experiments, but authors should address the attribution issues and presentation concerns in revision to maximize impact and accessibility.

It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. reviews.text:
# Peer Review: PAC Prediction Sets in Meta-Learning

## Summary

This paper presents a method for constructing prediction sets in meta-learning that come with PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) theoretical guarantees. The core contribution is a carefully designed framework that enables meta-test prediction sets to satisfy PAC bounds without requiring additional calibration. While the technical approach is sound and the experimental results are competitive, the paper suffers from significant presentation issues and limited experimental scope that prevent it from being a strong contribution in its current form.

## Strengths

1. **Strong Theoretical Foundation**: The provision of PAC bounds for meta-learning prediction sets is a valuable contribution that addresses an important gap in the literature. The careful design of the methodology to satisfy PAC settings is technically commendable.

2. **No Additional Calibration Required**: A key practical advantage of the proposed approach is that it delivers theoretical guarantees for meta-test prediction sets without requiring separate calibration procedures, which streamlines the workflow.

3. **Reasonable Experimental Coverage**: The method is evaluated across four datasets spanning three different application domains, demonstrating some breadth in the experimental validation.

4. **Competitive Performance**: The experimental results show that the method achieves competitive performance compared to existing baselines.

## Major Weaknesses

### 1. Presentation and Clarity Issues

The paper's presentation significantly hampers accessibility and understanding. The notation is confusing and often misleading, making it difficult for readers to follow the technical development. This is particularly problematic for a methodological contribution where clarity is essential for reproducibility and adoption.

### 2. Insufficient Background and Self-Containment

The paper relies heavily on Park et al. [1] without providing adequate background material. Readers unfamiliar with this prior work will struggle to understand the proposed method. The paper should dedicate several paragraphs to explaining the foundational concepts from Park et al. [1] to make the contribution more accessible.

### 3. Inadequate Explanation of Key Components

Critical elements of the framework are not clearly explained:
- **Figure 1 ambiguity**: The figure does not explicitly clarify that f is a pre-trained meta-model, which may confuse practitioners familiar with meta-learning terminology
- **Score function inconsistencies**: Score functions appear with different input spaces in different parts of the algorithm (e.g., lines 106 and 108) without explanation
- **Model-algorithm relationship**: The role of the meta-learned model and its relationship to the score function used in the PAC prediction set algorithm needs clarification

### 4. Limited Experimental Scope

Only a single meta-model architecture (prototypical networks) is used to evaluate the proposed method. This is a significant limitation that restricts our understanding of the method's generalizability and performance across different meta-learning approaches.

### 5. Incomplete Analysis of Practical Trade-offs

The paper does not clearly analyze the trade-off between prediction set error and set size when comparing Meta-PS and Meta-CP. Both metrics are reported, but their relative importance and the practical implications of the trade-offs are not discussed.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Limited Sensitivity Analysis**: The method uses PS-BINOM as the internal PAC prediction set algorithm, but the sensitivity to different estimators for τ is not evaluated. Understanding how different estimator choices affect performance would strengthen the empirical validation.

2. **Notation Clarity**: Beyond the major presentation issues, specific notation for score functions and prediction sets requires clarification throughout the paper.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Revise for Clarity**: Systematically improve the paper's presentation, paying particular attention to notation consistency and clarity.

2. **Enhance Figure 1**: Either modify Figure 1 or add explicit text in the main narrative clarifying that f represents a pre-trained meta-model and its role in the framework.

3. **Expand Background Section**: Add a substantial discussion of Park et al. [1] to make the paper self-contained for readers without deep familiarity with that work.

4. **Broaden Experimental Evaluation**: Evaluate the proposed method using multiple meta-model architectures beyond prototypical networks to demonstrate generalizability.

5. **Clarify Score Function Design**: Explain why score functions have different input spaces in different algorithm lines and discuss the implications for the overall framework.

6. **Deepen Trade-off Analysis**: Provide explicit discussion and visualization of the error-size trade-offs between Meta-PS and Meta-CP across different settings.

7. **Investigate Estimator Sensitivity**: Report results for different estimators of τ to understand the robustness of the approach to this design choice.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a technically sound contribution by extending PAC guarantees to the meta-learning setting. The theoretical framework is carefully constructed and the experimental results are encouraging. However, the work is significantly hampered by presentation issues that obscure the contribution and by experimental limitations that restrict our understanding of its practical applicability. The reliance on a single meta-model architecture and the lack of self-contained exposition of the foundational material are particular concerns.

Despite these limitations, the core contribution—providing PAC guarantees for meta-learning prediction sets—is valuable and the technical approach is solid. With substantial revisions addressing the presentation issues and expanded experimental validation, this could become a strong contribution to the field.

## Recommendation

**Accept with Major Revisions** (Borderline Accept)

The paper presents a technically sound method with meaningful theoretical guarantees. However, significant improvements in presentation, clarity, and experimental scope are necessary before publication. The authors should prioritize: (1) substantially improving the clarity and self-containment of the exposition, (2) broadening the experimental evaluation to include multiple meta-model architectures, and (3) providing clearer analysis of the practical trade-offs between different methods. These revisions are essential to make the contribution accessible and convincing to the broader community.

3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

title: Grounded Reinforcement Learning: Learning to Win the Game under Human Commands

reviews.summary:
Paper introduces a new task called “Grounded Reinforcement Learning” in which agents must be reward-maximizing with respect to some high-level language “constraint” (given by another agent/human). The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Results are not significantly better than baselines.
reviews.questions:
1) It is unclear how this approach is different from having standard RL with a combined state space over both command and game state.  As the command representation is not learnt - it seems unclear how this actually is different to a standard RL problem.

2) Can more justification be given to how difficult the opponent you play with is? Can you play with a harder agent to more clearly demonstrate the advantages of your method?

3) It is really unclear how the command token is meaningfully different to having an additional dimension within the game state. Can some justification be given to how either this representation is learnt or has any assemblance of language (is it compositional, have semantic or pragmatic properties).
reviews.limitations:
Addressing the above questions would be useful. 

I also believe actually using a language model would take great steps in demonstrating the effectiveness of your approach.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Grounded Reinforcement Learning

## Summary

This paper introduces Grounded Reinforcement Learning (GRL), a problem formulation where agents must simultaneously maximize rewards and comply with high-level language commands. The authors propose RED (Reinforced Demonstration Distillation), an algorithm that iteratively alternates between reinforcement learning and behavioral cloning phases. The approach is evaluated on the MiniRTS environment with both simulated agents and human evaluators.

## Strengths

The paper makes a valuable contribution by formalizing an important problem at the intersection of RL and language grounding. From a safety and human-compatible AI perspective, the work addresses a significant concern: ensuring that autonomous agents follow human directives while pursuing objectives. The presentation is clear and accessible, making the motivation and approach easy to follow.

The empirical evaluation is notably thorough. The human evaluation results are particularly compelling and provide convincing evidence that the approach produces behaviors that align with human expectations. Additionally, the authors provide comprehensive ablation studies that effectively justify their design choices, and the commitment to reproducibility through code release strengthens the contribution.

## Weaknesses and Concerns

Despite these strengths, several significant concerns limit the impact of this work:

**Language Grounding Claims**: The paper's use of "language" is somewhat misleading. Commands are encoded as one-hot representations without learned embeddings or properties that distinguish them from additional dimensions in the game state. The approach lacks fundamental components of natural language processing—pragmatics, syntax, and semantics are absent. An LSTM encoder is applied to command embeddings, but these are treated as fixed representations rather than learned natural language features. This raises a fundamental question: how does this differ meaningfully from standard RL with an augmented state space that includes a command dimension?

**Algorithmic Novelty**: The RED algorithm, while effective, combines two well-established techniques—reinforcement learning and behavioral cloning—in an alternating fashion. The core contribution appears to be primarily empirical validation of this combination rather than a novel algorithmic insight. The constrained RL formulation in Equation 2 may also be ill-posed, given the disjoint sample distributions between on-policy RL samples (where NA commands are used) and demonstration-based BC (where human commands are used). This distribution shift between on-policy and offline samples deserves more thorough treatment.

**Limited Empirical Gains**: The performance improvements over baselines are marginal. While the human evaluation is valuable, stronger empirical results would better support the practical utility of the approach.

**Evaluation Scope**: Testing is restricted to a single environment (MiniRTS) with simplified command sets. The paper would benefit from evaluation on harder opponents and more complex environments to better demonstrate the method's advantages. Additionally, there is no comparison with approaches leveraging pretrained language models, which would be a natural and competitive baseline for work claiming to address language grounding.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Clarify the Distinction**: Provide explicit justification for how this approach differs from standard RL with an augmented state space that includes command information as an additional dimension.

2. **Strengthen Language Grounding**: Demonstrate that the command representation exhibits properties of natural language, such as compositionality or semantic/pragmatic structure. Alternatively, consider integrating actual language models to better substantiate claims about language grounding.

3. **Address Distribution Shift**: More thoroughly analyze and address the distribution shift issue between on-policy RL samples and demonstration-based BC. This appears to be a potential source of training instability.

4. **Expand Evaluation**: Test the approach against stronger opponents and in more complex environments. Include comparisons with methods using pretrained language models.

5. **Provide Theoretical Justification**: Beyond empirical observations, offer theoretical or analytical insight into why the alternating RL-BC approach is effective for this problem.

## Minor Issues

- The representation of commands as one-hot vectors with an LSTM encoder should be more clearly distinguished from standard state augmentation approaches.
- The difficulty and sophistication of the MiniRTS opponent should be better characterized to contextualize the empirical results.

## Overall Assessment

This paper addresses an important problem and presents a well-executed empirical study with compelling human evaluation results. The work makes a solid contribution to the intersection of RL and human-compatible AI, with good experimental rigor and reproducibility. However, the claims regarding language grounding are overstated given that commands are treated as symbolic tokens rather than learned natural language representations. The algorithmic contribution is primarily empirical, combining existing techniques without substantial novelty. Despite these limitations, the clear presentation, thorough evaluation, and practical significance of the problem justify acceptance. The work would benefit from addressing the concerns raised above, particularly regarding the distinction from standard RL with augmented state spaces and the integration of genuine natural language processing components.

The paper makes sufficient contributions in problem formulation, evaluation methodology, and practical demonstration to merit publication, though it would be strengthened by more substantial algorithmic or theoretical innovations and broader empirical validation.

**Recommendation: Accept**

Clarify how the command token representation is meaningfully different from an additional dimension in the game state
Demonstrate that the command representation is learned or has properties of natural language (compositionality, semantic or pragmatic properties)
Consider using actual language models to better demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach
Address the distribution shift issue between on-policy samples and demonstrations more thoroughly
main_points: The paper conflates simple command conditioning with genuine language grounding
Commands are treated as one-hot representations rather than learned natural language representations
The distinction between this work and standard RL with augmented state space is unclear
RED's iterative RL-BC alternation lacks novelty as it combines existing techniques
Empirical improvements over baselines are marginal
The approach needs stronger validation on harder opponents and more complex environments
Theoretical understanding of why the method works is limited
technical_details: Commands are encoded as one-hot representations without learned embeddings or natural language properties
The approach lacks pragmatics, syntax, and semantic components of natural language
RED uses LSTM encoder for command embedding but this is treated as a fixed representation
The constrained RL formulation in Eq. 2 may be ill-posed due to disjoint sample distributions between RL (NA commands) and BC (human commands)
Distribution shift between on-policy samples and demonstrations causes training instability
The method is only evaluated on MiniRTS, which uses simplified command sets
No comparison with approaches using pretrained language models

reviews.summary:
This work focuses on grounded reinforcement learning. Generally, it means finding a conditional RL policy that can perfectly response to high-level command. 

In this paper, the authors proposed a constraned RL algorithm RED to (1) train an agent that is capable of winning strategy games and (2) follow most human instructions. Concretely, RED uses RL to learn explorative policy while uses BC to imitate human demonstration.

Experiment results suggest RED achieves command-following policies and higher winning rate.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths

* The idea is concise. Use BC to learn human behavior while use RL to explore better solution.
* Visualization and videos provided are mostly clear and contain necessary information.
* Fig1 is illustrative and helpful to explain what is grounded reinforcement learning. (It would be better if Fig 1 has no background color and in PDF format)
* Code is released.

### Weaknesses

* This work assumes reward from interactive environment. (limitation)
* The code has no detailed guidance and documentation.
* Though I like Figure 2 and other experiments, the main experiment results in Table 1 is confusing and non-convincing.
* The idea is not novel. Conditional imitation learning is a long lasting topics. Refer to "End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning"
* Many details are missing or confusing, such as Table 1 and Lagrangian method.
reviews.questions:
### Questions on method


Sec 4.3 says this work uses Lagrangian multiplier to solve the constrained optimization problem in Eq2. It turns out that, in my understanding, this is simply a weighted loss. A formal Lagrangian method should contain update process of the $\beta$, which is just ignored by this paper (or I miss, please point out). The later "iterative solution" also breaks the so called "Lagrangian method". 

---

Line 240 and Appendix A.2: Could you discuss more details on how you deal with action space? What does the "0/1 action for each unit" means? Who does the selection?

---

How to insert a NA command? IIUC, there exist a fixed set of possible commands right? So NA command should be just one more command in that set. You create embedding for each command in the set and than pass the embedding to the neural network.

---





### Questions on experiments

I can't understand table 1. IIUC, these four baselines have the policies that do not take command as input. Why they have different performance in Table 1 when applying different test commands? Please describe how you turn them as conditional policies. 

For example, since RL is trained without any objective to conduct distinguishable behaviors under different commands, why inserting different test commands will lead to different behavior?

---

I don't think Table 1 is convincing, especially when taking STD into account. 

Line 288: "Both RED and Joint policies substantially outperform Switch (BC) policy with sub-optimal commands (Random and Human Proxy), suggesting the conditioned behaviors are improved." This is a overclaimed statement. I can't draw this conclusion from Table 1.

---

I like Fig 2, which shows the dependency between commands and behaviors. Many "conditional RL" methods fail to prove that the conditional policy has casuality to the input command. This figure also shows that RED has much better "casuality" than RL.

My problem is that, in Figure 2, RL in 0% random commands achieves ~57% win rate. Does this value align with the RL performance with NA test command in Table 1?

Again, if RL is trained compelety without human command, then (1) where you can insert the command to the policy? and (2) why RL performance changes with the percentage of random commands?

---


Figure 6 says RL policy achieves similar win rate compared to RED. IIUC, RL policies are not conditioned on any human command. This result suggest that a human subject cooperates with RED trained conditional policy will lead to RL-comparable performance? This seems to be a violation to Table 1 "Oracle" result, which might lead to 

---

There is only one real gameplay footage provided (less than 2 minutes), with only 6 commands given, which is far less than convincing. Meanwhile, the commands seemed to be programmed/copy-pasted (I did not see any input lag), is there any real human intervention involved in recoding this video?

---

This game seems to be very complicated. Does your first intention of the application of this algorithm to be this high-level game? I understand the miniRTS environment is the only one that fitted the algorithm's need, but if you have alternatives (even if the performance is weak), would you also like to present them in the paper? Having diversity is a huge plus to your paper.








### Questions on paper




### Other questions or concerns



The [google drive link](https://drive.google.com/file/d/129ZV9CU6zUnp5B4v0p91Xaae8-M5zxHy/view) provided is **not anonymous**. This is a violation of the NeurIPS Code of Conduct (double-blind reviewing policy). Please action ASAP.

---

This paper has experiments involving 30 college students experiments, are attendees aware of their rights and responsibilities? A more explicit Ethics Statement should be included.

---

Conditional BC is not a new idea. Please refer to this work: End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning

---

Appendix D, as said in the checklist, has no information on human experiment. You moved them to Appendix E.

---

The website given in the appendix is very informative and I like the real-time gameplay footage, but there are several problems I want to point out:
- Code link is broken.
- Many grammar errors, will not be present here (plural or singular, tense, etc)
reviews.limitations:
The authors provide limitation section.

GRL is an interesting field. How to improve human-AI cooperation in test-time, how to learn a better human proxy, and how to further encourage, illustrate, quantify the causity between command and behaviors might be future directions.
